topics.name: Number System
topics.description: Study of different types of numbers (natural, whole, integers, rational, irrational) and their properties like divisibility, remainders, and factorization.
keyPoints: Prime Numbers: Divisible only by 1 and itself (2,3,5,7,11...)
Composite Numbers: Have more than 2 factors
Divisibility Rules: 2(even), 3(digit sum÷3), 4(last 2 digits÷4), 5(ends 0/5), 6(÷2 and ÷3), 8(last 3 digits÷8), 9(digit sum÷9), 11(alternating sum÷11)
formulas: Sum of first n naturals = n(n+1)/2
Sum of squares of first n naturals = n(n+1)(2n+1)/6
Sum of cubes of first n naturals = [n(n+1)/2]²
Number of factors of N = (a+1)(b+1)... where N = p₁ᵃ × p₂ᵇ

example.question: Find the number of factors of 360.
example.solution:
360 = 2³ × 3² × 5¹
Number of factors = (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4×3×2 = 24
Answer: 360 has 24 factors.

topics.name: Simplification & Approximation
topics.description: Simplification involves solving mathematical expressions using BODMAS rule. Approximation involves rounding numbers for quick mental calculation.
keyPoints: BODMAS: Brackets → Orders → Division → Multiplication → Addition → Subtraction
Vinculum (Bar) is solved first inside brackets
For approximation, round to nearest whole number or convenient value
formulas: Quick square: (a+b)² ≈ a² + 2ab when b is small

topics.name: HCF & LCM
topics.description: HCF (Highest Common Factor) is the greatest number that divides two or more numbers. LCM (Least Common Multiple) is the smallest number divisible by two or more numbers.
keyPoints: HCF: Find by prime factorization or division method
LCM: Find by prime factorization or common division
HCF divides the numbers; LCM is divisible by the numbers
formulas: HCF × LCM = Product of two numbers

example.question: Find HCF and LCM of 12 and 18.
example.solution:
12 = 2² × 3
18 = 2 × 3²
HCF = 2¹ × 3¹ = 6 (take minimum powers)
LCM = 2² × 3² = 36 (take maximum powers)
Verify: 6 × 36 = 216 = 12 × 18 ✓

topics.name: Ratio & Proportion
topics.description: Ratio compares two quantities. Proportion states that two ratios are equal. Used extensively in mixture, partnership, and age problems.
keyPoints: Proportion: a:b :: c:d means a/b = c/d
Mean proportional of a and b = √(ab)
Third proportional of a and b = b²/a
formulas: If a:b = c:d then ad = bc (cross multiplication)

example.question: If A:B = 3:4 and B:C = 5:6, find A:B:C.

topics.name: Percentage
topics.description: Percentage means 'per hundred'. It is used to express ratios, changes, and comparisons in a standardized way out of 100.
keyPoints: x% of y = y% of x
If A is x% more than B, then B is [x/(100+x)]×100% less than A
Successive discounts of a% and b% = (a + b - ab/100)%

example.question: A's salary is 20% more than B's. By what % is B's salary less than A's?
example.solution:
If B = 100, then A = 120
B is less by = (20/120) × 100 = 16.67%
Answer: B's salary is 16.67% less than A's.

topics.name: Profit & Loss
topics.description: Deals with buying and selling transactions. Profit when SP > CP, Loss when CP > SP.
keyPoints: CP = Cost Price, SP = Selling Price, MP = Marked Price
Discount is always on Marked Price
Profit/Loss is always calculated on Cost Price
If two items sold at same price, one at x% profit and other at x% loss: Net loss = x²/100 %

example.question: An article is marked at ₹500. After 20% discount, shopkeeper makes 25% profit. Find CP.

topics.name: Simple Interest
topics.description: Interest calculated only on the original principal amount. The interest remains constant every year.
keyPoints: SI is directly proportional to P, R, and T

example.question: Find SI on ₹8000 at 12% p.a. for 3 years.

topics.name: Compound Interest
topics.description: Interest calculated on principal + accumulated interest. Interest grows each period as it compounds.
keyPoints: CI > SI for same P, R, T (when T > 1)

example.question: Find CI on ₹10000 at 10% for 2 years compounded annually.

topics.name: Time & Work
topics.description: If a person can complete work in 'n' days, their 1 day's work = 1/n. Use LCM method for efficiency-based solutions.
keyPoints: LCM method: Total work = LCM of individual times

example.question: A can do a work in 12 days, B in 15 days. In how many days will they finish it together?
example.solution:
LCM(12,15) = 60 (total work)
A's efficiency = 60/12 = 5 units/day
B's efficiency = 60/15 = 4 units/day
Together = 9 units/day
Time = 60/9 = 6⅔ days

topics.name: Pipes & Cisterns
topics.description: Similar to Time & Work. Inlet pipe fills the tank (positive work), outlet pipe empties it (negative work).
keyPoints: Net work = Sum of all pipe rates
Use LCM method same as Time & Work

example.question: Pipe A fills a tank in 6 hours, Pipe B empties in 8 hours. If both are open, when will the tank fill?
example.solution:
LCM(6,8) = 24 (tank capacity)
A fills = 24/6 = 4 units/hr
B empties = 24/8 = 3 units/hr
Net = 4-3 = 1 unit/hr
Time = 24/1 = 24 hours

topics.name: Time, Speed & Distance
topics.description: Fundamental relationship: Distance = Speed × Time. Covers train problems, relative speed, and average speed concepts.
keyPoints: Relative speed: Same direction = difference, Opposite = sum

example.question: A train 150m long passes a pole in 15 seconds. Find its speed in km/h.
example.solution:
Speed = Distance/Time = 150/15 = 10 m/s
Convert: 10 × 18/5 = 36 km/h

topics.name: Boats & Streams
topics.description: Downstream (with current) speed increases, upstream (against current) speed decreases. Extension of speed-distance concepts.
keyPoints: Downstream: boat and stream in same direction

example.question: A boat goes 24 km downstream in 4 hours and 16 km upstream in 4 hours. Find speed of boat and stream.
example.solution:
Downstream speed = 24/4 = 6 km/h
Upstream speed = 16/4 = 4 km/h
Boat speed = (6+4)/2 = 5 km/h
Stream speed = (6-4)/2 = 1 km/h

topics.name: Average
topics.description: Average (Arithmetic Mean) is the sum of observations divided by number of observations.
keyPoints: When a new member joins: New avg changes
Weighted average when groups have different sizes
formulas: Average = Sum of all values / Number of values

example.question: Average of 5 numbers is 20. If one number is excluded, average becomes 18. Find the excluded number.
example.solution:
Total = 5 × 20 = 100
Remaining total = 4 × 18 = 72
Excluded number = 100 - 72 = 28

topics.name: Mixture & Alligation
topics.description: Alligation is a rule to find the ratio in which two or more ingredients at different prices/concentrations must be mixed.
keyPoints: Mean price lies between cheaper and dearer

example.question: Mix rice at ₹60/kg with rice at ₹90/kg to get mixture at ₹70/kg. Find the ratio.

topics.name: Partnership
topics.description: When two or more people invest money in a business, profit/loss is shared in the ratio of their investment×time.
keyPoints: Simple partnership: same time → profit ratio = investment ratio
Compound partnership: different times → ratio = capital × time
Working partner may get a salary + share of profit
formulas: A's share = A's ratio part / Total ratio × Total profit

example.question: A invests ₹5000 for 12 months, B invests ₹8000 for 6 months. Share ₹4500 profit.

topics.name: Problems on Ages
topics.description: Problems involving finding present, past, or future ages using linear equations. Age difference remains constant over time.
keyPoints: Ratio of ages changes over time
Set up linear equations with present age as variable
formulas: Age n years ago = Present - n
Age n years later = Present + n

example.question: Father is 3 times son's age. After 12 years, he'll be twice the son's age. Find their ages.
example.solution:
Let son = x, father = 3x
After 12: 3x+12 = 2(x+12)
3x+12 = 2x+24
x = 12
Son = 12 years, Father = 36 years

topics.name: Permutation & Combination
topics.description: Permutation is arrangement (order matters). Combination is selection (order doesn't matter).

example.question: In how many ways can 4 books be selected from 7 books?
example.solution:
This is selection (order doesn't matter)
⁷C₄ = 7!/(4!×3!)
= 5040/(24×6)
= 5040/144 = 35 ways

topics.name: Probability
topics.description: Probability measures the likelihood of an event occurring. Value ranges from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain).

example.question: Two dice are thrown. What is the probability that sum is 7?

topics.name: Mensuration (2D & 3D)
topics.description: Measurement of geometric shapes — area, perimeter, volume, surface area of 2D and 3D figures.
keyPoints: 2D: Area and Perimeter of triangle, circle, rectangle, etc.
3D: Volume, CSA, TSA of cube, cuboid, cylinder, cone, sphere

example.question: Find the volume of a cylinder with radius 7cm and height 10cm.

topics.name: Geometry
topics.description: Study of shapes, angles, and their properties. Includes triangles, circles, quadrilaterals, and coordinate geometry.
keyPoints: Exterior angle = Sum of remote interior angles

example.question: Find the distance between points (3,4) and (7,1).

topics.name: Trigonometry
topics.description: Study of relationships between angles and sides of triangles. Essential for height and distance problems.
formulas: sin = Opp/Hyp, cos = Adj/Hyp, tan = Opp/Adj

example.question: A tower is 50m tall. Find the angle of elevation from a point 50m from its base.
example.solution:
tan θ = Opposite/Adjacent = 50/50 = 1
θ = tan⁻¹(1) = 45°
Angle of elevation = 45°

topics.name: Algebra
topics.description: Deals with equations, identities, and algebraic expressions. Covers linear equations, quadratics, and identities.

example.question: Solve: x² - 7x + 12 = 0

topics.name: Logarithms
topics.description: Logarithm is the inverse of exponentiation. log_b(x) = y means b^y = x.
formulas: Change of base: log_a(b) = log b / log a

example.question: Find the value of log₂(32).

topics.name: Data Interpretation
topics.description: Interpreting data from tables, bar graphs, pie charts, and line graphs to answer questions.

example.question: If total expenditure is ₹50000 and food sector is 72° in a pie chart, find food expenditure.

topics.name: Data Sufficiency
topics.description: Determine whether given statements provide enough data to answer the question. You don't need to solve — just check sufficiency.

example.question:
Is x > 0?
(I) x² = 4
(II) x = -2
example.solution:
Statement I: x² = 4 → x = 2 or -2 → Not sufficient
Statement II: x = -2 → x < 0 → Sufficient to answer NO
Answer: Statement II alone is sufficient.

topics.name: Coding-Decoding
topics.description: A word or number is coded in a pattern. Decode using the pattern to find the answer.

example.question: If APPLE is coded as DSSOH, how is MANGO coded?
example.solution:
Each letter shifted by +3:
A→D, P→S, P→S, L→O, E→H
Similarly: M→P, A→D, N→Q, G→J, O→R
Answer: PDQJR

topics.name: Blood Relations
topics.description: Determine family relationships based on given information. Draw family trees for complex problems.

example.question: A is B's sister. C is B's mother. D is C's father. E is D's mother. How is A related to D?
example.solution:
E → D (mother-son)
D → C (father-daughter)
C → B (mother-child)
A is B's sister
So A is D's granddaughter.

topics.name: Direction Sense
topics.description: Track movement in different directions to find final position or distance from starting point.

example.question: A walks 3km North, then 4km East. How far from start?

topics.name: Number Series
topics.description: Find the pattern in a sequence of numbers and predict the next term or find the wrong term.

example.question: Find next: 2, 6, 12, 20, 30, ?
example.solution:
Differences: 4, 6, 8, 10, ?
Second differences: 2, 2, 2, 2
Next difference = 12
Answer: 30 + 12 = 42

topics.name: Alphabet Series
topics.description: Find patterns in sequences of letters or groups of letters.

example.question: Find next: AZ, BY, CX, DW, ?
example.solution:
First letter: A,B,C,D,E (increasing)
Second letter: Z,Y,X,W,V (decreasing)
Answer: EV

topics.name: Odd One Out
topics.description: Identify the item that doesn't belong to the group based on a common property.
keyPoints: Check if numbers: prime, even, perfect square
Check if words: category, spelling pattern

example.question: Find odd: 2, 5, 11, 17, 23, 27, 29
example.solution:
All are prime except 27 (27 = 3³)
Answer: 27 is the odd one out.

topics.name: Analogy
topics.description: Find a similar relationship: A is to B as C is to ?
keyPoints: Apply same relationship to find answer

example.question: Pen : Write :: Knife : ?
example.solution:
Pen is used to Write
Knife is used to Cut
Answer: Cut

topics.name: Classification
topics.description: Group items based on common properties and find the one that doesn't fit.

example.question: Group: Rose, Lotus, Jasmine, Carrot, Lily
example.solution:
Rose, Lotus, Jasmine, Lily are flowers.
Carrot is a vegetable.
Answer: Carrot doesn't belong.

topics.name: Syllogism
topics.description: Draw conclusions from given statements using Venn diagrams. Uses 'All', 'Some', 'No' type statements.
keyPoints: All A are B → draw A inside B
Some A are B → partial overlap
No A are B → no overlap

example.question: Statements: All dogs are animals. All animals are living. Conclusion: All dogs are living.
example.solution:
Dogs ⊂ Animals ⊂ Living
Since Dogs is inside Animals which is inside Living,
All dogs are living → TRUE ✓

topics.name: Statement & Conclusion
topics.description: Determine if the given conclusion logically follows from the statement.
keyPoints: Conclusion must follow from statement only
Check if conclusion is definitely true

example.question:
Statement: All employees must attend the meeting.
Conclusion: Raj, an employee, will attend.
example.solution:
Since ALL employees must attend and Raj is an employee,
Raj will attend.
Conclusion FOLLOWS. ✓

topics.name: Statement & Assumption
topics.description: An assumption is something taken for granted. Find what must be assumed for the statement to hold true.
keyPoints: Must be necessary for the statement
Should not be too broad or too narrow

example.question:
Statement: 'Buy XYZ soap for glowing skin'
Assumption: XYZ soap can make skin glow.
example.solution:
For the ad to make sense, it must be assumed that XYZ soap can provide glowing skin.
Assumption is VALID. ✓

topics.name: Seating Arrangement
topics.description: Arrange people in a row, circle, or around a table based on given conditions. Most important topic for bank exams.
keyPoints: Linear: Face same or opposite direction

example.question: A, B, C, D sit in a row facing north. B is to the right of A. C is between A and D. D is at the right end.
example.solution:
Starting from definite info: D is at right end.
B is right of A. C is between A and D.
Arrangement: A, B, C, D (left to right)
OR: A, C, B is not possible since C between A and D.
Answer: A B C D

topics.name: Puzzles
topics.description: Complex arrangement problems combining multiple parameters like floor, department, age etc.

example.question: 5 people live on 5 floors. A is above B. C is on floor 3. D is below C. E is on top floor.
example.solution:
E → Floor 5 (top)
C → Floor 3
D → Below C → Floor 1 or 2
A above B, remaining floors 2 and 4
If A=4, B=2 → D=1
Answer: D(1), B(2), C(3), A(4), E(5)

topics.name: Input-Output
topics.description: A machine rearranges numbers/words step by step. Find the pattern and predict outputs.
keyPoints: May involve both words and numbers

example.question:
Input: 45 based 17 glory 28
Step 1: 17 45 based glory 28
Step 2: 17 28 45 based glory
What is the pattern?
example.solution:
Numbers are being arranged in ascending order from left.
Step 1: Smallest number (17) moves to front
Step 2: Next smallest (28) moves to position 2
Pattern: Ascending sort of numbers, one per step.

topics.name: Ranking & Order
topics.description: Find a person's rank from top/bottom or position in a row/queue.
keyPoints: Rank from top + Rank from bottom = Total + 1

example.question: In a class of 40, Ram ranks 15th from top. What is his rank from bottom?
example.solution:
Rank from bottom = Total - Rank from top + 1
= 40 - 15 + 1 = 26th from bottom

topics.name: Inequalities
topics.description: Compare values using >, <, ≥, ≤, = symbols. Chain multiple relationships to derive conclusions.
keyPoints: A > B > C means A > C (transitive)
A ≥ B and B > C means A > C
A > B and B < C → can't compare A and C
Direction of inequality must be same to chain

example.question: If A > B ≥ C, D < B, E ≥ A. Which is definitely true? (a) E > C (b) D > C
example.solution:
E ≥ A > B ≥ C → E > C ✓
D < B ≥ C → D and C can't be compared
Answer: (a) E > C is definitely true.

topics.name: Decision Making
topics.description: Given a situation and criteria, decide the course of action. Tests judgment and reasoning ability.
keyPoints: Check each criterion one by one

example.question: Policy: Candidates must be 21-30 years, have 60%+ in graduation, and 2 years experience. A is 25, scored 72%, has 3 years experience.
example.solution:
Age: 25 (21-30) ✓
Graduation: 72% (≥60%) ✓
Experience: 3 years (≥2) ✓
Decision: Candidate A is SELECTED.

topics.name: Reading Comprehension
topics.description: Read a passage and answer questions based on it. Tests understanding, inference, and vocabulary in context.
keyPoints: Look for main idea in first/last paragraphs

example.question:
Passage discusses global warming effects.
Q: What is the main concern?
example.solution:
Read for central theme — the primary concern discussed throughout the passage.
Tip: Main idea is usually in the first paragraph or conclusion.

topics.name: Synonyms
topics.description: Find a word with the same or similar meaning.
keyPoints: Context matters — same word can have different meanings

example.question: Synonym of 'Benevolent':
example.solution:
Benevolent = Kind, generous, charitable
Answer: Kind/Generous

topics.name: Antonyms
topics.description: Find a word with the opposite meaning.
keyPoints: Think of the word's meaning first
Watch for tricky prefixes: un-, in-, dis-, ir-

example.question: Antonym of 'Frugal':
example.solution:
Frugal = being economical, spending little
Opposite = Extravagant, wasteful
Answer: Extravagant

topics.name: Sentence Correction
topics.description: Find and fix grammatical errors in sentences.

example.question: Correct: 'Each of the students have completed their work.'
example.solution:
'Each' is singular → verb should be 'has'
Correct: 'Each of the students has completed his/her work.'

topics.name: Error Detection
topics.description: Identify the part of the sentence that contains a grammatical error.

example.question: Find error: 'He is one of the best player (A) / in the team (B) / who has ever played (C) / No error (D)'
example.solution:
Part A: 'one of the best' requires plural → 'players'
Answer: Error in part A

topics.name: Fill in the Blanks
topics.description: Choose the correct word(s) to complete a sentence meaningfully.
keyPoints: Eliminate options that don't make sense

example.question:
The manager was _____ with the team's _____ performance.
(a) pleased, outstanding (b) angry, excellent
example.solution:
The sentence needs words that go together logically.
'pleased' goes with 'outstanding performance'
Answer: (a) pleased, outstanding

topics.name: Para Jumbles
topics.description: Rearrange jumbled sentences to form a coherent paragraph.
keyPoints: Look for pronouns — they refer to previously mentioned nouns

example.question: Arrange: A-India is growing. B-This growth is driven by IT. C-The economy has changed. D-Initially, agriculture dominated.
example.solution:
Logical order by flow:
D (Initially, agriculture) → C (economy changed) → A (India growing) → B (driven by IT)
Answer: DCAB

topics.name: Cloze Test
topics.description: A passage with blanks to fill. Tests vocabulary and contextual understanding.

example.question: The _____ of technology has _____ the way we communicate.
example.solution:
The 'advancement/evolution' of technology has 'transformed/changed' the way we communicate.
Pick words that fit both grammar and meaning.

topics.name: Idioms & Phrases
topics.description: Common English expressions with figurative meanings different from literal interpretation.
keyPoints: Use in context to remember better

example.question: Meaning of 'Hit the nail on the head':
example.solution:
Meaning: To describe exactly what is causing a situation.
Example: 'You hit the nail on the head when you said the problem is poor communication.'

topics.name: One Word Substitution
topics.description: Replace a phrase or description with a single word.

example.question: A person who loves books:
example.solution:
Bibliophile (biblio = book, phile = lover)
Other examples:
Misanthrope = one who hates mankind
Philanthropist = one who loves mankind

topics.name: Active & Passive Voice
topics.description: Convert between active voice (subject does action) and passive voice (action is done to subject).
keyPoints: Passive: Object + be + Past Participle + by Subject
Not all sentences can be converted

example.question: Convert to passive: 'The cat chased the mouse.'
example.solution:
Active: The cat (S) chased (V) the mouse (O)
Passive: The mouse was chased by the cat.
Tense: Simple Past → was/were + V3

topics.name: Direct & Indirect Speech
topics.description: Convert between quoted speech and reported speech with appropriate tense and pronoun changes.

example.question: Convert: He said, 'I am going home.'
example.solution:
He said that he was going home.
Changes: 'I' → 'he', 'am going' → 'was going', removed quotes, added 'that'

topics.name: Vocabulary
topics.description: Building a strong vocabulary is essential for all verbal ability sections. Learn new words daily.
keyPoints: Learn word roots for guessing meanings

example.question: Word: Ephemeral
example.solution:
Meaning: Lasting for a very short time
Synonym: Transient, fleeting, temporary
Antonym: Permanent, enduring
Usage: 'The beauty of cherry blossoms is ephemeral.'

topics.name: Tables
topics.description: Data presented in rows and columns. Calculate totals, averages, percentages, and ratios from tabular data.
keyPoints: Read row and column headers carefully

example.question: A table shows sales of 5 products across 4 quarters. Find which product had highest average quarterly sales.
example.solution:
Calculate average for each product:
Product A: (10+15+12+13)/4 = 12.5
Compare averages across all products to find the highest.

topics.name: Bar Graphs
topics.description: Data shown as rectangular bars. Height/length represents value. Can be vertical, horizontal, or stacked.
keyPoints: Stacked bars: difference between sections = individual value

example.question: Bar graph shows exports from 2018-2022. In which year was the growth rate highest?
example.solution:
Calculate year-over-year growth rate:
Growth% = (Current-Previous)/Previous × 100
Compare all years to find maximum growth rate.

topics.name: Pie Charts
topics.description: Circular chart divided into sectors. Each sector represents a proportion. Total = 360° or 100%.

example.question: Total budget = ₹2,00,000. Education sector = 54°. Find amount spent on education.

topics.name: Line Graphs
topics.description: Data points connected by lines showing trends over time. Used to identify patterns, peaks, and changes.
keyPoints: Compare parallel lines for relative performance

example.question: Line graph shows temperature over 7 days. On which day was the rise maximum?
example.solution:
Check day-to-day increase:
Day 1→2: 25→28 = +3
Day 2→3: 28→27 = -1
Find the day with maximum positive change.

topics.name: Caselets
topics.description: Data given in paragraph form rather than visual charts. Extract numbers and organize into tables before solving.
keyPoints: Read carefully and extract all numbers

example.question: A company has 500 employees. 60% are male. 30% of males and 40% of females are in IT dept. How many are in IT?
example.solution:
Males = 60% of 500 = 300
Females = 500-300 = 200
Males in IT = 30% of 300 = 90
Females in IT = 40% of 200 = 80
Total in IT = 90+80 = 170

topics.name: Data Comparison
topics.description: Compare two quantities and determine the relationship between them without necessarily calculating exact values.
keyPoints: Options: A>B, A<B, A=B, Can't determine
Use estimation rather than exact calculation

example.question:
Quantity A: 15% of 240
Quantity B: 25% of 140
example.solution:
A = 15% × 240 = 36
B = 25% × 140 = 35
A > B
Answer: Quantity A is greater.

topics.name: Basic Mathematics
topics.description: Fundamental math concepts including arithmetic operations, fractions, decimals, and number properties.

example.question: Simplify: 3/4 + 2/5

topics.name: Basic Statistics
topics.description: Mean, median, mode, range, variance, and standard deviation — fundamental statistical measures.

example.question: Find mean, median, mode of: 3, 5, 7, 5, 9, 5, 11
example.solution:
Sorted: 3, 5, 5, 5, 7, 9, 11
Mean = 45/7 ≈ 6.43
Median = 5 (4th value in 7 items)
Mode = 5 (appears 3 times)

topics.name: Logical Data Analysis
topics.description: Combining logical reasoning with data analysis. Solve problems using both reasoning and mathematical calculations.

example.question: Three friends A, B, C scored differently in 3 subjects. Total of each is same. A scored highest in Math. B scored lowest in Science.
example.solution:
Create a grid and use constraints:
Step 1: Assign possible scores
Step 2: Use 'A highest in Math' to fix Math scores
Step 3: Use 'B lowest in Science' to fix Science scores
Step 4: Fill remaining ensuring equal totals
